title: Evaluating the Reward Model
concept: Evaluate the reward model by checking how often its scores match human preference rankings on a validation set. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO).'

exercises.prompt: How do we evaluate if a reward model is doing a good job?

options.text: By comparing its scores against human preference rankings
By counting how many words it writes per second
By checking if it can connect to the internet

exercises.explain: A reward model's job is to mimic human preferences, so we evaluate it by comparing its scores to human rankings on test data.

cards.title: The Ultimate Test
cards.body: We have trained our reward model to score AI answers, but how do we know we can trust it? Before we let it teach our main AI, we must put it through a final exam using human rankings.

cards.title: Reward Model Evaluation
cards.body: Evaluating a reward model means measuring how often it agrees with human preferences on a brand new validation dataset. If the model scores the human-preferred answer higher than the rejected answer, it gets a point for accuracy!

cards.title: The Gymnastics Judge
cards.body: Imagine you are training a gymnastics judge. To test them, you show them videos of past routines that senior judges already ranked. If your trainee judge ranks the routines in the same order as the senior judges, they pass the test! Evaluating a reward model works the same way.

cards.title: Checking the Scores
cards.body: Suppose we have a validation set where humans prefer Answer A over Answer B. If our reward model gives Answer A a score of 8.5 and Answer B a score of 3.2, it successfully matches the human preference. If it did the opposite, we know it needs more training.

cards.title: Perfect Scores vs. Right Order
cards.body: You might think a reward model needs to score answers perfectly from 0 to 100. In reality, the exact numbers don't matter as much as the relative ranking. As long as the preferred answer gets a higher score than the bad one, the model is doing its job!

cards.title: Evaluation Comparison
cards.body: Let's compare a successful evaluation run versus a failed evaluation run when checking our reward model.

right.text: Gives higher scores to rejected choices or ranks them completely randomly.

left.text: Gives higher scores to human-preferred choices (e.g., Prefers helpful tips over spam).

exercises.prompt: What is the main metric we look at when evaluating a reward model?

options.text: How fast the model generates new sentences.
How often the model's scores match human preference rankings.
The total number of words in the validation dataset.
Whether the model can translate text into different languages.

exercises.explain: Reward model evaluation is about checking if the model gives higher scores to the outputs that human evaluators preferred.

exercises.prompt: True or False: We should evaluate the reward model using the exact same data we used to train it.
exercises.explain: Just like in school, testing a model on data it has already seen (training data) doesn't prove it can handle new, unseen situations. We must use a separate validation set.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the description of reward model testing.
exercises.sentence: To check if the reward model works on new data, we test it on a validation set.
exercises.explain: We use a validation set (data the model hasn't seen during training) to get an honest measure of how well our reward model generalizes to new preferences.

exercises.prompt: Which of these reward model outputs correctly matches human preferences?

options.text: AI Response A (Preferred): 9.2 points | AI Response B (Rejected): 4.1 points
AI Response A (Preferred): 2.0 points | AI Response B (Rejected): 7.5 points
AI Response A (Preferred): 5.0 points | AI Response B (Rejected): 5.0 points

exercises.explain: Option B shows the reward model failing because it scored the rejected response higher than the preferred one. Option C has equal scores, which is a tie, not a clear match. Option A correctly ranks the preferred response higher.

exercises.prompt: How do we ultimately decide if our reward model is ready to guide our LLM?

options.text: We let it generate new text and hope for the best.
We calculate how often its scores match the ranking order of human preference validation data.
We train another reward model to evaluate the first one.
We write hardcoded math formulas to replace the human judges.

exercises.explain: Great job! You've learned how to evaluate a reward model. By testing it against human validation rankings, we make sure it acts as a reliable guide before the AI starts its training. Next up: Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO).
